INTRODUCTION


ERMAN MELVILLE, the author of “ Moby Dick,”
was born in New York City, August 1,1819. He
came of excellent American stock, for through his
father, Allan Melville, he was descended from

pre-Revolutionary Scotch-Irish settlers in New England,
and through his mother, Maria Gansevoort, from the earli-
est Dutch colonists in New York. Both of his grand-
fathers had distinguished themselves in the war of the
Revolution. Restless and inquisitive by nature, the young
Herman tried his hand at various occupations, working in
a bank, clerking in his brother’s store, farming and teaching
school before he went to sea as a cabin-boy on a vessel
trading to Liverpool. On returning to the United States
he taught school again; but the lure of wandering still
drew him—as he himself wrote: “I love to sail forbidden
seas, and land on barbarous coasts ”—and he shipped for a
voyage on a whaler bound for the South Seas. In those seas
and on those little-known islands, of which Melville was
the first competent literary artist to write, he had many
- adventures, making friends with native chiefs and mis-
sionaries, spending a period as the prisoner of cannibals,—
of whom he said that “a more humane, gentlemanly and
amiable set of epicures do not probably exist in the Pa-
cific,’—learning much about tribes that few white men had
visited. His voyage home was made on the frigate United
States, on which he shipped as a sailor, and so acquired
experience of life aboard an American man-of-war.

On his return from three years of whaling and South
Sea drifting and adventures in the Navy Melville was
besought by his friends to put his adventures into a book.
This he did, and his volume “ 'Typee” was published in
1846,


INTRODUCTION


>


The wanderer now married and settled down to write
further books, in which romance was mixed with actual
experiences at sea and in foreign lands. He made his
home first in New York City and then at Pittsfield in the
Berkshire Hills of Western Massachusetts. He died in
1891.

“ Moby Dick ” is generally considered Herman Melville’s
greatest book, its narrative power and description of
natural history being superior to the earlier stories
“'Typee ” and “ Omoo.” The scope of the story may per-
haps not be seen in the first few chapters, but there is
wonderful movement and suspense and passion in this
dramatic portrayal of the pursuit of the great whale by the
intrepid Yankee captain and his crew.

MOBY - DICK
Or, The White Whale


CHAPTER I
LOOMINGS


ALL me Ishmael. Some years ago—never mind how
long precisely—having little or no money in my
purse, and nothing particular to interest me on
shore, I thought I would sail about a little and see

the watery part of the world. It is a way I have of driving
off the spleen, and regulating the circulation. Whenever I
find myself growing grim about the mouth; whenever it is a
damp, drizzly November in my soul; whenever I find myself
involuntarily pausing before coffin warehouses, and bring-
ing up the rear of every funeral I meet; and especially
whenever my hypos get such an upper hand of me, that it
requires a strong moral principle to prevent me from delib-
er
